title: Salsa on Carpet
stainChemistry: Salsa is a four-component stain: (1) TOMATO base (lycopene pigment + mild tannin + citric acid), (2) CHILI (capsaicin suspended in oil + capsanthin red pigment — both FAT-SOLUBLE, not water-soluble), (3) ONION + GARLIC (sulfur compounds + trace protein), (4) CILANTRO / SPICES (plant tannin + trace pigment). The unique element versus other tomato-based stains: capsaicin and capsanthin are fat-soluble pigments carried in oil. Water-only cleaning doesn't touch them — they stay in the fiber after the tomato red rinses away, leaving a lingering orange / red ghost. The correct order: scrape solids → cold water flush (removes citric acid, loose tomato) → DAWN to strip the oil (critical — releases fat-soluble pigments) → peroxide to oxidize lycopene + capsanthin → vinegar rinse. Skipping the oil-strip step leaves capsanthin locked behind.
whyThisWorks: Cold water removes the water-soluble fraction fast. Dawn emulsifies the chili oil and releases the fat-soluble red pigment. Only THEN can peroxide reach and oxidize the pigment. Vinegar rinse keeps tannin on the acid side. Correct order = clean. Wrong order (peroxide before Dawn) = oil blocks oxidizer = orange ghost.
defaultAssumption: Game-day / taco-Tuesday / Mexican takeout spill on living-room or kitchen carpet. Fresh + caught fast = reasonable win. Dried in = harder because of capsaicin-oil penetration. Restaurant salsa tends to be oilier than jarred (bigger oil-strip job). Store-bought jarred salsa has more vinegar and less oil (easier).

spottingProtocol.instruction: Scoop tomato chunks, onion, cilantro into a garbage bag. Don't rub.
Pour small amount of cold water, blot. Repeat 2–3 times. Removes loose tomato + citric acid. Cold only. Don't flood.

spottingProtocol.agent: Dawn + Soft Brush — Strip the Oil
spottingProtocol.technique: CRITICAL step to release fat-soluble pigment
spottingProtocol.instruction: Work Dawn into the spot with soft toothbrush 5–10 min. Dawn emulsifies chili oil and releases capsanthin. Skip this and peroxide can't reach the pigment. Damp-cloth rinse.
Dab peroxide on hidden carpet area. Color transfer = skip peroxide. Clean = proceed.
3% peroxide on damp cloth, 5–10 min, blot. 2–3 cycles for restaurant-style salsa. Don't mix with ammonia / bleach / alkaline.

spottingProtocol.equipment: 1 tbsp vinegar + 1 cup cool water on damp cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: Vinegar rinse on damp cloth, 5 min. Neutralizes peroxide, keeps tannin light. Blot dry.
Weighted paper towels overnight. Fan-dry. Inspect daylight.

steps: 3. Dawn + soft brush oil-strip — CRITICAL step.
warnings: Oil-strip BEFORE oxidizer — chili pigment is fat-soluble
NEVER alkaline cleaners on tomato tannin
NEVER hot water — cross-links tannin, caramelizes any sugar
Hidden peroxide test mandatory on dyed carpet

homeSolutions.agent: Why Salsa Leaves a Lingering Orange Ghost
homeSolutions.instruction: Salsa is tomato + onion + CHILI OIL + spices. The chili part — capsaicin and the red pigment capsanthin — is FAT-SOLUBLE. Water-only cleaning doesn't touch them. They stay in the carpet fiber after the tomato red rinses away, leaving an orange ghost. That's why a lot of "cleaned" salsa spots still have a faint orange tint. The fix is simple: strip the oil with Dawn BEFORE you use peroxide. Order matters.
Spoon or plastic card to scrape tomato chunks, onion, cilantro into a garbage bag. Don't rub. Don't wipe. Just scoop.

Pour a SMALL amount of cold water on the spot and blot with a dry cloth. Repeat 2–3 times. Removes loose tomato pigment and citric acid. Cold only — hot water cross-links tannin and sets pigment. Don't flood — pad wicking is the enemy.

homeSolutions.agent: Dawn + Soft Toothbrush — OIL STRIP (The Key Step)
homeSolutions.instruction: Work a drop of Dawn with cool water into the spot using a soft toothbrush for 5–10 minutes. This is the step most home guides skip and it's why salsa leaves a ghost. Dawn emulsifies the chili oil and releases the fat-soluble red pigment so the next step (peroxide) can reach it. Without this step, oil blocks peroxide and the orange stays. Damp-cloth rinse.
In a hidden carpet area, dab 3% hydrogen peroxide on a white cloth, press 2 minutes. Color transfer = skip peroxide. Clean = proceed.
Dab 3% drugstore peroxide on a damp white cloth, press on the spot 5–10 minutes, blot. Oxidizes lycopene (tomato red) and capsanthin (chili red). Repeat 2–3 times for restaurant-style oilier salsa.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix peroxide with ammonia, chlorine bleach, OxiClean, or baking soda.

homeSolutions.instruction: Mix 1 tablespoon white vinegar + 1 cup cool water. Dab on damp cloth, press 5 min. Neutralizes peroxide, keeps remaining tannin on the light (acidic) side of its color equilibrium. Blot dry. Don't skip this — it prevents the ghost from returning as a brownish ring.

Stack dry paper towels on the spot, heavy book on top, leave overnight. Fan-dry in the morning. Prevents pigment wick-back. Inspect in daylight. Residual orange = one more Dawn → peroxide → vinegar round.

homeSolutions.agent: Escalate for Wool or Ghost That Won't Move
homeSolutions.instruction: Call a pro if the carpet is wool (pH-sensitive — pro chemistry safer), if someone used baking soda / OxiClean before you got there (tannin locked), or if an orange ghost persists after two full rounds with proper oil-strip first. Tell them: "Salsa on [nylon / polyester / wool] carpet, age [fresh / overnight], home-treated in order: cold flush → Dawn oil-strip → 3% peroxide → vinegar. No alkaline used. [Residual orange ghost / locked tannin]."

steps: 1. Know the four components — oil-strip before peroxide is the key.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if baking soda / OxiClean was already used (tannin locked), if the carpet is wool and peroxide fails hidden test, or if orange ghost persists after two rounds with proper oil-strip sequence.
warnings: Oil-strip BEFORE peroxide — chili pigment is fat-soluble
NEVER alkaline cleaners on tomato tannin
Hidden peroxide test mandatory on dyed carpet
Never mix peroxide with bleach / ammonia / alkaline

neverDo: Never skip Dawn oil-strip before peroxide
Never mix peroxide with bleach / ammonia
fiberSensitivities: Nylon / polyester / olefin: tolerates full protocol
Wool: pH-sensitive — pro lane for tight spots
Dyed carpet: hidden peroxide test mandatory

materialWarnings: Chili pigment (capsanthin) is FAT-SOLUBLE — oil-strip or ghost remains
Order matters more than product choice

escalation.when: Alkaline cleaner was used before (tannin locked), wool carpet, orange ghost persists after two full rounds with correct oil-strip order.
escalation.whatToTell: "Salsa on [nylon / polyester / wool] carpet, age [fresh / overnight], home-treated in order: cold flush → Dawn oil-strip → 3% peroxide [× X cycles] → vinegar. No alkaline used. [Orange ghost / locked tannin / fiber-test failed]."
escalation.specialistType: professional carpet cleaner with combination-stain chemistry

contextQuestions: Mild, medium, or hot? (hot = more capsaicin oil)
Has anyone used alkaline cleaner already?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Restaurant salsa has more chili oil than jarred. Hot salsa has more capsaicin than mild. Oilier + hotter = bigger fat-soluble pigment load = Dawn step is even more critical.
